subject: Mathematics
q: Let the equation of the pair of lines, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>p</mi><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>q</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> can be written as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>p</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>q</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Then the equation of the pair of the angle bisectors of the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is:
question: Let the equation of the pair of lines, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>p</mi><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>q</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> can be written as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>p</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>q</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Then the equation of the pair of the angle bisectors of the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is:
solution:
We have, equation of a pair of lines x2-4xy-5y2=0

Now, equation of bisector of a pair of lines x2-y21--5=xy-2

⇒x2-y26=xy-2

⇒x2-y2=-3xy

⇒x2+3xy-y2=0.

subject: Mathematics
q: A triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> lying in the first quadrant has two vertices as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. If<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>B</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>90</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mo>,</mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>ar</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>Δ</mi><mi>ABC</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> sq. units, then the abscissa of the vertex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is :
question: A triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> lying in the first quadrant has two vertices as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. If<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>B</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>90</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mo>,</mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>ar</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>Δ</mi><mi>ABC</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> sq. units, then the abscissa of the vertex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is :
solution:
mAC=β−2α−1

mAB=2−11−3=−12

AB⊥AC

∴ β−2α−1−12=−1

β=2α−2+2

β=2α

Now area of ΔABC=55=12AB.AC

⇒ 123−12+1−22.α−12+β−22=55

⇒ α−12+2α−22=10

⇒ α−125=10

⇒ α−1=25

⇒ α=1±25.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the points of intersections of the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> are the mid points of the sides of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ABC</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ABC</mi></math> is
question: Let the points of intersections of the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> are the mid points of the sides of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ABC</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ABC</mi></math> is
solution:
If P, Q, R are mid-points of sides of ∆ABC then  4×Area(ΔPQR)=Area(∆ABC)

Area of  ΔABC=4×12a1 b1c1a2 b2c2a3 b3c32C1C2C3 

Where C1,C2,C3 are cofactors of c1,c2,c3

 ΔABC=4×121-111-232-5112(-1)(3)(-1)

=2×9÷3

=6

subject: Mathematics
q: The distance of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo></math> from the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>28</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, measured parallel to the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, is equal to
question: The distance of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo></math> from the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>28</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, measured parallel to the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, is equal to
solution:
Let, L1:3x-y+1=0 and L2:2x-3y+28=0.

Slope, m1=3=tanθ

⇒θ=60°

⇒sinθ=32, cosθ=12

Now, equation of line passing through 2,3 and having slope 3 is,

x-212=y-332=r

⇒2x-3y+28=0

⇒4+r-9-332r+28=0

⇒2-332r+23=0

⇒r=4633-2×33+233+2

⇒r=4623×33+21=4+63

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the equation $\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}+2)(12-\mathrm{k})=2$ have equal roots. Then the distance of the point $\left(\mathrm{k}, \frac{\mathrm{k}}{2}\right)$ from the line $3 x+4 y+5=0$ is
question: Let the equation $\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}+2)(12-\mathrm{k})=2$ have equal roots. Then the distance of the point $\left(\mathrm{k}, \frac{\mathrm{k}}{2}\right)$ from the line $3 x+4 y+5=0$ is
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \left(\mathrm{x}^2+2 \mathrm{x}\right)(12-\mathrm{k})=2 \\ & \lambda \mathrm{x}^2+2 \lambda \mathrm{x}-2=0 \quad \mathrm{k} \neq 12 \text { Let } 12-\mathrm{k}=\lambda \\ & \mathrm{D}=0 \\ & 4 \lambda^2+8 \lambda=0 \\ & \lambda=0 \text { or } \lambda=-2 \\ & \Rightarrow 12-\mathrm{k}=-2 \\ & \mathrm{k}=14
\end{aligned}$
$\text { So } P\left(k, \frac{k}{2}\right)=(14,7)$
$\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{3 \times 14+4 \times 7+5}{5}\right|=15$
option (1)

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let the lines $3 x-4 y-\alpha=0,8 x-11 y-33=0$, and $2 x-3 y+\lambda=0$ be concurrent. If the image of the point
$(1,2)$ in the line $2 x-3 y+\lambda=0$ is $\left(\frac{57}{13}, \frac{-40}{13}\right)$, then $|\alpha \lambda|$ is equal to
question:
Let the lines $3 x-4 y-\alpha=0,8 x-11 y-33=0$, and $2 x-3 y+\lambda=0$ be concurrent. If the image of the point
$(1,2)$ in the line $2 x-3 y+\lambda=0$ is $\left(\frac{57}{13}, \frac{-40}{13}\right)$, then $|\alpha \lambda|$ is equal to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \because \mathrm{PM}=\mathrm{QM} \\ & \text { So, } \mathrm{M}\left(\frac{\frac{57}{13}+1}{2}, \frac{\frac{-40}{13}+2}{2}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{35}{13}, \frac{-7}{13}\right) \end{aligned}$ $\because$ M lies on the line $\begin{gathered}2 x-3 y+\lambda=0 \\ 2\left(\frac{35}{13}\right)-3\left(\frac{-7}{13}\right)+\lambda=0 \\ \lambda=-\frac{70}{13}+\frac{21}{13} \\ =\frac{-91}{13}=-7 \\ \left|\begin{array}{ccc}3 & -4 & -\alpha \\ 8 & -11 & -33 \\ 2 & 3 & \lambda\end{array}\right|=0\end{gathered}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 3(-11 \lambda-99)+4(8 \lambda+66)-\alpha(-24+22)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 33 \lambda-297+32 \lambda+264+24 \alpha-22 \alpha=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-\lambda+2 \alpha-33=0 \\ & \therefore \lambda=-7 \\ & -(-7)+2 \alpha-33=0 \\ & 2 \alpha=26 \\ & \alpha=13 \\ & \therefore|\alpha \lambda|=|13 \times(-7)| \\ & =91 \end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the line $L_1 : x + 3 = 0$ intersect the lines $L_2 : x - y = 0$ and $L_3 : 3x + y = 0$ at the points $A$ and $B$, respectively. Let the bisector of the obtuse angle between the lines $L_2$ and $L_3$ intersect the line $L_1$ at the point $C$. Then $BC^2 : AC^2$ is equal to:
question: Let the line $L_1 : x + 3 = 0$ intersect the lines $L_2 : x - y = 0$ and $L_3 : 3x + y = 0$ at the points $A$ and $B$, respectively. Let the bisector of the obtuse angle between the lines $L_2$ and $L_3$ intersect the line $L_1$ at the point $C$. Then $BC^2 : AC^2$ is equal to:
solution:
The intersection point of $L_1: x = -3$ and $L_2: x - y = 0$ is $A(-3, -3)$.

The intersection point of $L_1: x = -3$ and $L_3: 3x + y = 0$ is $B(-3, 9)$.

The lines $L_2$ and $L_3$ intersect at the origin $O(0, 0)$.

The distances from the origin to $A$ and $B$ are:
$OA = \sqrt{(-3)^2 + (-3)^2} = 3\sqrt{2}$
$OB = \sqrt{(-3)^2 + 9^2} = 3\sqrt{10}$

The angle $\angle AOB$ between the segments $OA$ and $OB$ can be determined using the dot product of vectors $\overrightarrow{OA}$ and $\overrightarrow{OB}$:
$\overrightarrow{OA} \cdot \overrightarrow{OB} = (-3)(-3) + (-3)(9) = 9 - 27 = -18$

Since the dot product is negative, $\cos(\angle AOB) < 0$, which means $\angle AOB$ is an obtuse angle. Therefore, the internal bisector of $\angle AOB$ is the bisector of the obtuse angle between the lines $L_2$ and $L_3$.

According to the internal angle bisector theorem in $\triangle AOB$, the bisector of $\angle AOB$ divides the opposite side $AB$ at point $C$ in the ratio of the adjacent sides:
$\dfrac{AC}{BC} = \dfrac{OA}{OB} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{3\sqrt{10}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$

Squaring both sides yields:
$\dfrac{AC^2}{BC^2} = \dfrac{1}{5} \Rightarrow \dfrac{BC^2}{AC^2} = 5$

Thus, $BC^2 : AC^2 = 5 : 1$.

Answer: $5:1$

subject: Mathematics
q: A straight line cuts off the intercepts $\mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{a}$ and $\mathrm{OB}=\mathrm{b}$ on the positive directions of $\mathrm{x}$-axis and $\mathrm{y}-$ axis respectively. If the perpendicular from origin $\mathrm{O}$ to this line makes an angle of $\frac{\pi}{6}$ with positive direction of $y$-axis and the area of $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$ is $\frac{98}{3} \sqrt{3}$, then $\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{b}^2$ is equal to:
question: A straight line cuts off the intercepts $\mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{a}$ and $\mathrm{OB}=\mathrm{b}$ on the positive directions of $\mathrm{x}$-axis and $\mathrm{y}-$ axis respectively. If the perpendicular from origin $\mathrm{O}$ to this line makes an angle of $\frac{\pi}{6}$ with positive direction of $y$-axis and the area of $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$ is $\frac{98}{3} \sqrt{3}$, then $\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{b}^2$ is equal to:
solution:
Plotting the diagram of the given data we get,

Equation of straight line

Here given OA=a & OB=b, so equation of line AB in intercept form will be xa+yb=1 ......1

And given perpendicular line of length p is making π3 angle with the x-axis,

So, the equation of line in normal form will be

xcosπ3+ysinπ3=p

⇒x2p+y32p=1

So, on comparing with the equation 1 we get,

a=2p & b=2p3

Now area of the triangle OAB is 

12×a×b=9833

⇒12×2p×2p3=9833

⇒p2=49

Therefore,

a2-b2=4p2-4p23=83p2

⇒a2-b2=8349=3923

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>|</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>|</mo><mo>≠</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, be points such that the area of triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> sq. unit, then the sum of all possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is:
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>|</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>|</mo><mo>≠</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, be points such that the area of triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> sq. unit, then the sum of all possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is:
solution:
Given coordinates of triangle ABC, A(a,0),B(b,2b+1),C(0,b)

Area=12a01b2b+110b1

12a(b+1)+b2=±1

ab+a+b2=±2

If  2 is positive 

a=2−b2b+1

If  2 is negative

a=−2−b2b+1

Sum of possible values of a=2-b2b+1+-2-b2b+1

=-2b2b+1

subject: Mathematics
q: The set of all possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> for which the points<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> lie on the same side of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is?
question: The set of all possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> for which the points<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> lie on the same side of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is?
solution:
We know that if Ax1,y1 & Bx2,y2 lies on the same side to line L=0, then LAx1,y1 LBx2,y2>0.

Given, the points A1,2 & Bsinθ,cosθ lies on the same side of the line x+y-1=0.

∴1+2-1sinθ+cosθ-1>0

⇒sinθ+cosθ>1

⇒sinθ×12+cosθ×12>1×12

⇒sinθ.cosπ4+cosθ.sinπ4>12

⇒sinθ+π4>12

⇒π4<θ+π4<3π4

⇒0<θ<π2

⇒θ∈0,π2

subject: Mathematics
q: In a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo></math> the co-ordinates of the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> respectively. If the equation of the perpendicular bisector of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the centre of the circumcircle of the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is:
question: In a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo></math> the co-ordinates of the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> respectively. If the equation of the perpendicular bisector of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the centre of the circumcircle of the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is:
solution:
Perpendicular bisector of PR is 2x-y+2=0 

Midpoint of PQ is M1,1

Equation of perpendicular bisector of PQ is y=x.

Point of intersection of equations PR and PQ is circumcentre.

So, circumcentre is -2,-2

subject: Mathematics
q: A ray of light passing through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> reflects on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and the reflected ray passes through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> be the point that divides the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> internally into the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. Let the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> on the bisector of the angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>β</mi></math> is equal to _____.
question: A ray of light passing through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> reflects on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and the reflected ray passes through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> be the point that divides the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> internally into the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. Let the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> on the bisector of the angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>β</mi></math> is equal to _____.
solution:
By observation we know the point Aα,0 and β=y-coordinate of R=2×4+1×02+1=83     ⋯1

Now P' is image of P in y=0 which will be P'2,-3

∴  Equation of P'Q is y+3=4+35-2x-2 

i.e. 3y+9=7x-14

Now P'Q meets x-axis at A

so A≡237,0 by solving with y=0

∴α=237     ⋯2

By 1,2, we get

7α+3β=23+8=31

subject: Mathematics
q: A light ray emerging from the point source placed at $\mathrm{P}(1,3)$ is reflected at a point $\mathrm{Q}$ in the axis of $x$. If the reflected ray passes through the point $R$ $(6,7)$, then the abscissa of $Q$ is:
question: A light ray emerging from the point source placed at $\mathrm{P}(1,3)$ is reflected at a point $\mathrm{Q}$ in the axis of $x$. If the reflected ray passes through the point $R$ $(6,7)$, then the abscissa of $Q$ is:
solution:
Let abcissa of $\mathrm{Q}=x$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & Q=(x, 0) \\ & \tan \theta=\frac{0-7}{x-6}, \tan \left(180^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\frac{0-3}{x-1}\end{aligned}$ Now, $\tan \left(180^{\circ}-\theta\right)=-\tan \theta$ $$ \therefore \quad \frac{-3}{x-1}=\frac{-7}{x-6} \Rightarrow x=\frac{5}{2} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the pair of straight lines $x^2-2 p x y-y^2=0$ and $x^2-2 p x y-y^2=0$ be such that each pair bisects the angle between the other pair, then
question: If the pair of straight lines $x^2-2 p x y-y^2=0$ and $x^2-2 p x y-y^2=0$ be such that each pair bisects the angle between the other pair, then
solution:
Equation of bisector of both pair of straight lines, $p x^2+2 x y-p y^2=0$
$q x^2+2 x y-q y^2=0$
From (1) and (2). $\frac{\mathrm{q}}{1}=\frac{2}{-2 \mathrm{p}}=\frac{-\mathrm{q}}{-1} \Rightarrow \mathrm{pq}=-1$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the line <math><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>24</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> intersects the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis is at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the incentre of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mo> </mo></math>is the origin, is:
question: If the line <math><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>24</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> intersects the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis is at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the incentre of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mo> </mo></math>is the origin, is:
solution:
The x intercept of the line 3x+4y-24=0 can be obtained by putting y=0 i.e. 3x-24=0

⇒x=8

Hence, the point A is 8, 0.

Now, the y intercept of the line 3x+4y-24=0 can be obtained by putting x=0 i.e. 4y-24=0

⇒y=6

Hence, the point B is 0, 6.

In ∆OAB, we have OA=b=8, OB=a=6 and AB=c=8-02+0-62=64+36=10 units.

And, we know that the co-ordinates of incentre I of a triangle with vertices x1, y1, x2, y2 and x3, y3 and having length of the sides opposite to these vertices as a, b and c, respectively,  is

I≡ax1+bx2+cx3a+b+c, ay1+by2+cy3a+b+c

⇒I≡68+80+10024, 60+86+10024

⇒I=2, 2.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> be non-zero numbers. If the point of intersection of the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>d</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> lies in the fourth quadrant and is equidistant from the two axes then
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> be non-zero numbers. If the point of intersection of the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>d</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> lies in the fourth quadrant and is equidistant from the two axes then
solution:
Let (h,k) be the intersection of the two given lines its equidistant from both axes.

∴ h=k

Since it lies in the fourth quadrant, the point can be written as (h,-h), where h>0.

(h,-h) lies on both the lines.

∴ 4ah-2ah+c=0

∴ c=-2ah   ∴ h=-c2a

5bh-2bh+d=0

∴ d=-3bh   ∴ h=-d3b

∴ -c2a=-d3b

∴ 3bc-2ad=0

subject: Mathematics
q: If the orthocentre of the triangle formed by the lines $y=x+1, y=4 x-8$ and $y=m x+c$ is at $(3,-1)$, then $\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{c}$ is :
question: If the orthocentre of the triangle formed by the lines $y=x+1, y=4 x-8$ and $y=m x+c$ is at $(3,-1)$, then $\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{c}$ is :
solution:
Solve line PQ \& QR Point $\mathrm{Q}\left(\frac{1-\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{m}-1}, \frac{1-\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{m}-1}+1\right)$
$m_{2 H}=\frac{\frac{1-c}{m-1}+2}{\frac{1-c}{m-1}-3}=\frac{1-c+2 m-2}{1-c-3 m+3}=-\frac{1}{4}$
$\mathrm{m}_{2 \mathrm{H}}=\frac{\frac{1-\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{m}-1}+2}{\frac{1-\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{m}-1}-3}=\frac{1-\mathrm{c}+2 \mathrm{~m}-2}{1-\mathrm{c}-3 \mathrm{~m}+3}=-\frac{1}{4}$...(1)
$\because \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{PH}}=\frac{5}{0} \rightarrow \infty$
$\Rightarrow$ Slope of line QR (m) $=0$
Put value of $m$ in equation (1)
$\frac{1-c-2}{1-c+3}=-\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow c=0$
so $\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{c}=0$ Ans.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the straight lines $x+3 y=4,3 x+y=4$ and $x+y$ $=0$ form a triangle, then the triangle is
question: If the straight lines $x+3 y=4,3 x+y=4$ and $x+y$ $=0$ form a triangle, then the triangle is
solution:
Let equation of $A B: x+3 y=4$
Let equation of $B C: 3 x+y=4$
Let equation of $C A: x+y=0$
Now, By solving these equations we get $A=(-2,2), B=(1,1)$ and $C=(2,-2)$
Now, $A B=\sqrt{9+1}=\sqrt{10}$,
$$
B C=\sqrt{1+9}=\sqrt{10}
$$
and $C A=\sqrt{16+16}=\sqrt{32}$
Since, length of $A B$ and $B C$ are same
therefore triangle is isosceles.

subject: Mathematics
q: The sides of a rhombus $A B C D$ are parallel to the lines, $x-y+2=0$ and $7 x-y+3=0$. If the diagonals of the rhombus intersect at $P(1,2)$ and the vertex $A$ (different from the origin) is on the $y$ axis, then the ordinate of $A$ is
question: The sides of a rhombus $A B C D$ are parallel to the lines, $x-y+2=0$ and $7 x-y+3=0$. If the diagonals of the rhombus intersect at $P(1,2)$ and the vertex $A$ (different from the origin) is on the $y$ axis, then the ordinate of $A$ is
solution:
Let the coordinate $A$ be $(0, c)$
Equations of the given lines are
$x-y+2=0$ and
$7 x-y+3=0$
We know that the diagonals of the rhombus will be parallel to the angle bisectors of the two given lines; $y=x+2$ and $y=7 x+3$
$\therefore$ equation of angle bisectors is given as:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{x-y+2}{\sqrt{2}}=\pm \frac{7 x-y+3}{5 \sqrt{2}} \\
&5 x-5 y+10=\pm(7 x-y+3)
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Parallel equations of the diagonals are $2 x$ $+4 y-7=0$ and $12 x-6 y+13=0$
$\therefore$ slopes of diagonals are $\frac{-1}{2}$ and 2 .
Now, slope of the diagonal from $A(0, \mathrm{c})$ and passing through $P(1,2)$ is $(2-c)$
$$
\therefore 2-c=2 \Rightarrow c=0 \text { (not possible) }
$$

$$
\therefore 2-c=\frac{-1}{2} \Rightarrow c=\frac{5}{2}
$$

$$
\therefore \text { ordinate of A is } \frac{5}{2} \text {. }
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: In a triangle $A B C$, coordianates of $A$ are $(1,2)$ and the equations of the medians through $B$ and $C$ are $x+y=5$ and $x=4$ respectively. Then area of $\triangle A B C$ (in sq. units) is
question: In a triangle $A B C$, coordianates of $A$ are $(1,2)$ and the equations of the medians through $B$ and $C$ are $x+y=5$ and $x=4$ respectively. Then area of $\triangle A B C$ (in sq. units) is
solution:
Median through $C$ is $x=4$ So the $x$ coordianate of $C$ is 4 . let $C \equiv(4, y)$, then the midpoint of $A(1,2)$ and $C(4, y)$ is $D$ which lies on the median through $B$.
$$ \therefore \quad D \equiv\left(\frac{1+4}{2}, \frac{2+y}{2}\right) $$ Now $\frac{1+4+2+y}{2}=5 \Rightarrow y=3$. So, $C \equiv(4,3)$. The centroid of the triangle is the intersection of the mesians. Here the medians $x=4$ and $x+4$ and $x+y=5$ intersect at $G(4,1)$. The area of triangle $\triangle A B C=3 \times \triangle A G C$ $$ =3 \times \frac{1}{2}[1(1-3)+4(3-2)+4(2-1)]=9 . $$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $A(h, k), B(1,1)$ and $C(2,1)$ be the vertices of a right angled triangle with $A C$ as its hypotenuse. If the area of the triangle is $1$, then the set of values which ' $\mathrm{k}$ ' can take is given by
question: Let $A(h, k), B(1,1)$ and $C(2,1)$ be the vertices of a right angled triangle with $A C$ as its hypotenuse. If the area of the triangle is $1$, then the set of values which ' $\mathrm{k}$ ' can take is given by
solution: $\frac{1}{2} \times 1(k-1)=\pm 1$ $k-1=\pm 2$ $k=3$ $k=-1$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><mi>S</mi></math> be the set of all triangles in the <math><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></math> -plane, each having one vertex at the origin and the other two vertices lie on coordinate axes with integral coordinates. If each triangle in <math><mi>S</mi></math> has area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> sq. units, then the number of elements in the set <math><mi>S</mi></math> is:
question: Let <math><mi>S</mi></math> be the set of all triangles in the <math><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></math> -plane, each having one vertex at the origin and the other two vertices lie on coordinate axes with integral coordinates. If each triangle in <math><mi>S</mi></math> has area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> sq. units, then the number of elements in the set <math><mi>S</mi></math> is:
solution:
Since the vertex of given triangle is O (origin) and other two vertices A and B lies on the coordinate axes, hence triangle must be a right-angled triangle.

Area =12x.y                        ( x, y∈I)

As per given condition

12x.y=50⇒x. y=100

Possible ordered pairs of x, y when ∆ formed in 1st quadrant are 9 i. e. 1,100, 2,50, 4,25, 5,20, 10,10, 25, 4, 50, 2, 100,1

∆ can be formed in any of the quadrants, hence total number of cases 9×4=36.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the area of a $\triangle P Q R$ with vertices $P(5,4), Q(-2,4)$ and $R(a, b)$ be 35 square units. If its orthocenter and centroid are $O\left(2, \frac{14}{5}\right)$ and $C(c, d)$ respectively, then $c+2 d$ is equal to
question: Let the area of a $\triangle P Q R$ with vertices $P(5,4), Q(-2,4)$ and $R(a, b)$ be 35 square units. If its orthocenter and centroid are $O\left(2, \frac{14}{5}\right)$ and $C(c, d)$ respectively, then $c+2 d$ is equal to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Equation of lines } Q R=5 x+2 y+2=0 \\ & \text { Equation of lines } P R=10 x-3 y-38=0 \\ & \therefore \text { Point } R(2,-6) \\ & \text { Centroid }=\left(\frac{5-2+2}{3}, \frac{4+4-6}{3}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{5}{3}, \frac{2}{3}\right) \\ & c+2 d=\frac{5}{3}+\frac{4}{3}=3\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be vertices of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is the circumcentre of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, then which of the following is NOT correct about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be vertices of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is the circumcentre of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, then which of the following is NOT correct about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>
solution:
Given,

Aα,-2, Bα,6 & Cα4,-2

Now slope of AC=0 and slope of AB=80→∞

Now we can see AC is perpendicular to AB.

So, ∆ABC is right angled at A.

So, Circumcentre =mid point of BC=5α8,2

Now given circumcentre ≡5,α4

So on comparing we get,5α8=5 & α4=2

⇒α=8

Now plotting the diagram and finding the sides with distance formula we get,

Area =1268=24

Perimeter =24

Circumradius =102=5

Inradius =Δs=2412=2

subject: Mathematics
q: Let \(A B C\) be a triangle formed by the lines \(7 x-6 y+3=0, x+2 y-31=0\) and \(9 x-2 y-19=0\). Let the point \((h, k)\) be the image of the centroid of \(\Delta A B C\) in the line \(3 x+6 y-53=0\). Then \(h^2+k^2+h k\) is equal to:
question: Let \(A B C\) be a triangle formed by the lines \(7 x-6 y+3=0, x+2 y-31=0\) and \(9 x-2 y-19=0\). Let the point \((h, k)\) be the image of the centroid of \(\Delta A B C\) in the line \(3 x+6 y-53=0\). Then \(h^2+k^2+h k\) is equal to:
solution:
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { centroid of } \triangle \mathrm{ABC} & =\left(\frac{9+3+5}{3}, \frac{11+4+13}{3}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{17}{3}, \frac{28}{3}\right)\end{aligned}$
Let image of centroid with respect to line mirror is $\begin{aligned} & (\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k}) \\ & \therefore\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}-\frac{28}{3}}{\mathrm{~h}-\frac{17}{3}}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=-1 \\ & \& 3\left(\frac{\mathrm{~h}+\frac{17}{3}}{2}\right)+6 \cdot\left(\frac{\frac{\mathrm{k}+28}{3}}{2}\right)=53 \end{aligned}$ Solving (1) \& (2) we get $\mathrm{h}=3, \mathrm{k}=4$ $\therefore \mathrm{h}^2+\mathrm{k}^2+\mathrm{hk}=37$

subject: Mathematics
q: A square, of each side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>, lies above the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and has one vertex at the origin. If one of the sides passing through the origin makes an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the positive direction of the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis , then the sum of the <math><mi>x</mi></math>-coordinates of the vertices of the square is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>:</mo></math>
question: A square, of each side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>, lies above the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and has one vertex at the origin. If one of the sides passing through the origin makes an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the positive direction of the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis , then the sum of the <math><mi>x</mi></math>-coordinates of the vertices of the square is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>:</mo></math>
solution:
For A,

xcos30°=ysin30°=2

⇒x=232= 3 and y=1

For C,

xcos120°=ysin120°=2

x=-1, y=3

For B,

xcos75°=ysin75°=22

x= 3-1, y=3+1

Sum of the x-coordinates =0+3+3-1+-1

=23-2

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $L$ be the line $y=2 x$, in the two dimensional plane.
Statement 1: The image of the point $(0,1)$ in $L$ is the point $\left(\frac{4}{5}, \frac{3}{5}\right)$
Statement 2: The points $(0,1)$ and $\left(\frac{4}{5}, \frac{3}{5}\right)$ lie on opposite sides of the line $\mathrm{L}$ and are at equal distance from it.
question:
Let $L$ be the line $y=2 x$, in the two dimensional plane.
Statement 1: The image of the point $(0,1)$ in $L$ is the point $\left(\frac{4}{5}, \frac{3}{5}\right)$
Statement 2: The points $(0,1)$ and $\left(\frac{4}{5}, \frac{3}{5}\right)$ lie on opposite sides of the line $\mathrm{L}$ and are at equal distance from it.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not a correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is a correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.

subject: Mathematics
q: A point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> moves on the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> are fixed points, then the locus of the centroid of <math><mi>Δ</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is a line:
question: A point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> moves on the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> are fixed points, then the locus of the centroid of <math><mi>Δ</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is a line:
solution:
Let, coordinate of centroid is (h, k) and coordinate of point P is (α, β)

We know that the centroid of a triangle with vertices x1, y1, x2, y2 and x3, y3 is x1+x2+x33, y1+y2+y33.

Hence, in ∆PQR, we have α+1+33=h and β+4-23=k

⇒α=3h-4, β=3k-2

Since (α, β) lies on the line 2x-3y+4=0

Hence, 23h-4-33k-2+4=0

⇒6h-9k+2=0

For finding the locus replace h, k by x, y

Hence, the locus of centroid is 6x-9y+2=0 which has slope =-6-9=23 since the slope of a line ax+by+c=0 is -ab.

subject: Mathematics
q: A light ray emits from the origin making an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the positive $x$-axis. After getting reflected by the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, if this ray intersects x-axis at Q, then the abscissa of Q is
question: A light ray emits from the origin making an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the positive $x$-axis. After getting reflected by the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, if this ray intersects x-axis at Q, then the abscissa of Q is
solution:
On plotting the diagram of given data we get,

Let Q(h,0)

∵OP reflected by x+y=1

So, the image of Q lies on y=x3

∴ x-h1=y1=-2(h-1)2

⇒x=1, y=1-h

Since it lies on y=x3

∴ 1-h=13

⇒h=1-13=3-13

On rationalising the numerator we get,

⇒h=3-13×3+13+1

⇒h=23+3

q: If the point α, 733 lies on the curve traced by the mid-points of the line segments of the lines x cosθ+ysinθ=7, θ∈0, π2 between the co-ordinates axes, then α is equal to
question: If the point α, 733 lies on the curve traced by the mid-points of the line segments of the lines x cosθ+ysinθ=7, θ∈0, π2 between the co-ordinates axes, then α is equal to
solution:
The straight line is shown below with the intercepts.

M72cosθ, 72sinθ≡h, k

∴ cosθ=72h    sinθ=72k

∵ θ∈0, π2

⇒494h2+494k2=1   ∴ x and y are positive

∴ Locus =1x2+1y2=449

Now α, 733 lies on curve

⇒1α2+9147=449

1α2=149

∴ α=±7

∴ α=7

Hence this is the required option.
